Lotte sat on the edge of the museum workbench, her back to the Rijksmuseum’s conservation lab. The overhead lights hummed, casting a sterile glow over the workbenches lined with magnifiers and brushes. She held a small transistor radio in one hand, the volume low, just loud enough to catch the words.

A man’s voice, calm and rehearsed: “…after reviewing all available evidence, I must clarify that my earlier statements to Ms. Laurent were based on assumptions, not facts. The allegations regarding Dutch Art Dealers Association’s involvement in illicit activities are unfounded and damaging to the reputation of the entire art community.”

Lotte’s thumb hovered over the power button.

She didn’t kill the radio.

She let it play.

The voice continued, unspooling excuses like silk, wrapping around Sophie’s credibility until it choked. Lotte could practically see Sophie in her apartment, hands clenched, jaw set—battling the same suffocation she’d felt years ago when Victor’s men had dragged her name through the mud.

She turned the radio off.

Silence settled over the lab. Only the distant hum of air filtration and the occasional creak of the old building breathing.

Lotte set the radio down. She stared at her reflection in the polished steel surface of the workbench. Her face was calm. Empty. The way she’d trained it to be.

She knew that voice.

She knew who’d spoken.

She also knew Sophie wouldn’t stop. Not yet. Not ever.

Lotte exhaled, slow and quiet.

Then—

A knock at the lab door.

She turned.

Daan stood in the doorway, his coat damp at the shoulders, his dark hair plastered to his forehead. He held a manila folder in one hand, unzipped his coat with the other. Water dripped from the fabric onto the polished floor.

“Can I come in?”

Lotte didn’t move. “You always did.”

He stepped inside, closed the door behind him. The sound of it clicking shut echoed.

“You heard,” he said.

She crossed her arms. “Heard what?”

“The recantation. On every channel.” He held up the folder. “Victor wants me to bring you in. Off the record. Immunity in exchange for the files.”

Lotte’s gaze flicked to the folder. Then to his face. “Immunity from what?”

“Everything.” He stepped closer. “Lotte, this ends now. If you give him the files, he’ll make it disappear. Your name. The past. It’s over.”

She tilted her head. “Over for who?”

Daan’s jaw tightened. “For you. For Sophie. For anyone who gets caught in the crossfire.”

She held his eyes. “And you? Is it over for you?”

His silence was answer enough.

She turned away, walked to the far end of the bench, picked up a fine-haired brush. She ran her thumb along the bristles, feeling the slight give.

“You’re scared,” she said. “Victor’s withdrawing. Galleries are pulling funding. People are asking questions. And now your star witness just turned on the one person who could bring him down.”

Daan stepped closer. “I’m not scared of Victor.”

“Then why didn’t you come to me three days ago?”

“I was gathering leverage. Proof. Something permanent.”

She set the brush down. Turned. “You mean the files. The ones you’ve been sitting on for two years.”

His face darkened. “I had to protect myself.”

“By protecting him.”

“By keeping you alive.”

The words hung between them, raw and unguarded.

Lotte exhaled through her nose. “You always did love playing the hero. Just never for the right side.”

Daan’s hand twitched. He wanted to reach for her. She could see it in the way his fingers curled, the way his chest rose, held.

She stepped back.

“Don’t,” she said. “Not now. Not like this.”

He swallowed. “Then what? You’re just going to walk away? Let Sophie take the fall? Let Victor bury you again?”

“I’m not walking away.” She picked up the radio, unplugged it, set it on the bench. “I’m staying. Watching. Waiting.”

Daan stared at her. “You’re going to expose him.”

“I already am.”

He shook his head. “You don’t have enough. Not without the files.”

She smiled. Just a flicker. “I have everything I need right here.” She tapped her temple.

Daan’s expression twisted. Anger. Frustration. Guilt.

She turned away. “You can tell Victor I’m available. Any time. But I won’t negotiate with a man who’s already lost.”

He didn’t move.

She didn’t look back.

The silence stretched. The air between them thickened with everything unsaid.

Then—

Daan’s coat pocket vibrated.

A single pulse. Low. Insistent.

His hand went to his pocket.

Lotte watched from the corner of her eye as he pulled out his phone. The screen lit up his face, casting sharp shadows.

He read the message. His breath caught.

Then he looked at her.

“It’s Victor,” he said.

She crossed her arms. Waited.

Daan’s thumb hovered over the screen. He didn’t reply.

Lotte stepped closer. “What does he want?”

Daan swallowed. “Your location.”

She smiled. “Tell him I’m right where he left me.”

Daan’s thumb descended.

She didn’t watch him press send.

She walked to the far side of the lab, to the window overlooking the museum courtyard. The rain had stopped, but the cobblestones glistened. A delivery bike wheeled past, its basket empty, its rider hunched against the cold.

She looked back at Daan.

He stood frozen, phone still in hand, staring at the screen.

Then—

A chime. Incoming call.

Not a text. Not a message.

A call.

Daan’s eyes flicked to hers, wide and panicked.

Lotte didn’t wait.

She picked up the nearest metal stool and hurled it across the lab.

It struck the door with a metallic crash, sending vibrations through the hinges. Daan flinched.

The phone kept ringing.

She didn’t answer.

She just stood there, watching as Daan’s hesitation curdled into something darker.

Something irreversible.

The call went to voicemail.

Daan slipped the phone back into his pocket.

But the damage was done.

Lotte’s breath came slow, even.

She looked at him one last time.

Then she walked past him, out of the lab, and into the hallway beyond.

The door swung shut behind her.

Daan didn’t follow.

Chapter 9

Rain tapped a staccato rhythm against the bricked-over window of De Bakkerswinkel, Amsterdam’s oldest pastry shop. The scent of cinnamon and yeast hung thick in the damp air, clinging to Lotte’s coat as she exhaled. She didn’t wipe the steam from the glass. Let it blur everything. Let it hide her.

Daan’s silhouette appeared beyond the fogged pane—a lean shape moving through the gray light. He paused under the awning of the next shop, shoulders hunched against the cold. A hesitation. Two. Then he turned toward the alley.

Lotte didn’t move from her place by the counter. A half-eaten apple tart sat congealing on a white plate. She hadn’t touched it.

The bell above the door chimed.

Daan stepped inside, water dripping from his sleeves onto the checkerboard tiles. He shook the rain from his hair like a dog shaking off water. His eyes found her immediately.

She sipped her coffee. Black. Bitter. Just how he used to take it.

He didn’t greet her. Didn’t ask permission. Just walked over, pulled out the stool across from her, and sat.

The pastry shop was empty except for them. The baker in the back hummed off-key, kneading dough with the kind of rhythm that made time feel irrelevant.

“You look well,” Daan said.

Lotte set the cup down. “Liar.”

A flicker of something—annoyance, maybe—crossed his face. He unzipped his coat, draped it over the back of the stool. His shirt underneath was damp at the collar. He hadn’t bothered with an umbrella.

“I mean it,” he said. “The museum suits you.”

“It’s not the museum.” She gestured to the white apron tied over her clothes. “It’s the coat.”

He almost smiled. Almost.

She nudged the apple tart toward him. “Want some?”

He didn’t touch it. “Victor wants to talk.”

“Victor always wants to talk.”

“Not like this. He’s offering immunity.”

Lotte picked up a fork, tapped the edge against the plate. A hollow *click*. “Immunity from what?”

“Everything.” Daan leaned in. “The files. The past. The name. It all goes away.”

She finally looked at him. Really looked. The lines around his eyes were deeper. The dark circles beneath them were new.

“Does it?” she asked. “Or does it just get buried under a new name, a new scandal, a new set of lies?”

Daan’s jaw tightened. “I’m not asking for forgiveness. I’m asking you to survive.”

“Survive what? Another decade in the shadows? Another decade watching Victor collect trophies while I clean his dirty laundry?”

He exhaled sharply through his nose. “He’s not the same man.”

“No,” she said. “He’s worse.”

Silence settled between them, thick as the steam from their drinks. Outside, a tram rumbled past, its brakes hissing.

Daan reached into his coat pocket. Pulled out a manila folder. Slid it across the table.

Lotte didn’t open it.

“What’s in there?” she asked.

“A statement. A signed affidavit. Victor’s willing to admit the 2018 deal was a mistake. That the paperwork was forged. That the Rembrandt sketch in your bench—” He stopped. Swallowed. “That it was never yours.”

Lotte’s fingers curled into her palms. “You mean the one *he* forged. The one *he* planted. The one that got me exiled.”

Daan didn’t deny it. Just waited.

She tapped the folder. “And in exchange?”

“Your silence. Your files. Everything.”

“Everything?” She laughed, a dry sound. “Including your conscience?”

He didn’t rise to it. “I’m offering you a way out.”

“You’re offering me a way to disappear.” She pushed the folder back toward him. “Again.”

He shoved it forward. “Take it.”

“Why?” She leaned back. “So Victor can wave it in Sophie’s face? So he can call *her* the liar? So he can keep his precious market clean while he drowns in the blood of everyone who helped him?”

Daan’s hand clenched into a fist on the table. “Sophie’s already drowning. You heard the recantation. Her source just called her a witch hunter on live TV.”

“And you think that changes anything?” Lotte’s voice was low, steady. “Sophie doesn’t care about Victor’s approval. She cares about the truth. And you just handed him the sledgehammer to smash her with.”

Daan’s fingers twitched. “I’m trying to fix this.”

“You’re trying to save yourself.”

He went very still.

She pressed on. “You’re the one who told Victor where to find me years ago. You’re the one who let him destroy me. You’re the one who—” She stopped. Swallowed. “You’re the one who hasn’t slept since you saw me at the museum.”

Daan’s breath came out shaky. “I did what I had to.”

“No.” She shook her head. “You did what you *chose*. Just like you chose him now.”

He looked away, toward the window where rain streaked the glass. “I chose survival.”

“Survival?” She laughed again. “You think this is survival? Watching your mentor rot? Watching your lover burn? Watching your own hands shake every time the phone rings?” She leaned forward. “I survived. I’m still standing. And I didn’t have to sell my soul to do it.”

Daan’s eyes flicked back to hers. Something raw passed between them—guilt, longing, regret. All tangled up in the steam and the scent of sugar.

She reached out. Not to touch him. Just to close the folder.

“Take it back,” she said.

He didn’t move.

“Victor’s losing,” she said. “Galleries are pulling out. Buyers are asking questions. The foundation’s crumbling.” She tapped the folder. “This isn’t an offer. It’s a desperate grab at a life preserver. And I’m not drowning with him.”

Daan’s voice dropped. “What are you going to do?”

She stood. Slid her chair back. The legs scraped against the tile.

“I’m going to do what I should have done three years ago,” she said. “I’m going to end him.”

He didn’t stop her.

She pulled her coat from the back of the stool, draped it over her shoulders. The rain had lightened to a mist.

As she turned toward the door, Daan spoke.

“He knows where you are.”

Lotte paused. Didn’t look back.

“He always does,” she said.

The door chimed behind her as she stepped into the alley.

Rain slicked the cobblestones. The alley smelled of old brick and wet cardboard. She walked to the end, where the path opened onto the canal-side street. A bike leaned against a post. No one in sight.

She leaned against the wall, breathing in the cold air.

Her phone buzzed in her pocket.

She pulled it out. Unknown number.

The screen lit up.

She answered.

“Lotte.”

Victor’s voice. Smooth. Imperious.

“You’re making a mistake.”

She didn’t reply.

“Daan offered you a chance to walk away.” His tone shifted, coaxing. “I’m offering you the same. But I won’t be so generous next time.”

She tapped the phone against her palm. “Next time?”

“When I find you.” A pause. Then, quieter: “You’re not as invisible as you think.”

The line went dead.

She stared at the screen. The call ended. The rain blurred the letters.

She slipped the phone back into her pocket.

Then she walked toward the canal. Not running. Not hiding.

Just moving.

Chapter 10

The Rijksmuseum restoration lab smelled of turpentine and quiet desperation. Lotte sat at her bench, angled toward the north window where Rembrandt’s *Night Watch* hung in its dignified dimness. The real one, not a copy. The one she would never forge again.

Her laptop screens glowed in the low light—three of them, stacked in a V, the cables snaking across the zinc work surface like veins. The encryption app hummed, the only sound besides the occasional drip of water from the faucet across the room.

She tapped the spacebar. The files advanced.

Each one was labeled. Dates. Titles. A single letter beside each: *L*.

Her monogram. Not a confession. A brand.

The first file opened. A Vermeer landscape—one of hers, sold in 2021 through a Zurich gallery. The digital signature matched hers. The provenance, altered by Victor’s people. The buyer’s name redacted.

She scrolled. Metadata. Bank transfers. All routed through shell companies in the Caymans.

She tapped the next. A fake Mondrian grid, 2019. Again, her signature, her brushstrokes enhanced in the file’s metadata. Victor’s signature wasn’t here.

Not yet.

She exhaled through her nose. No time to savor this. Not here.

The door to the lab creaked open.

Lotte didn’t turn. She minimized the window, letting the screensaver—a loop of her own restoration work—hide the incriminating files. She reached for a soft cloth, began polishing a clean brush handle. The motion was automatic, the kind of thing she did to look busy.

Footsteps. Light, precise. A woman’s.

“Lotte.”

Sophie Laurent stood in the doorway, her dark coat damp at the hem, her hair pinned back in a messy bun. She held a paper coffee cup, the lid slightly askew. A smudge of lipstick on the rim.

Lotte kept polishing. “You’re early.”

“Traffic was light.” Sophie stepped inside, shut the door behind her with her hip. “No one followed me. I checked.”

“Good.”

Sophie set the cup on the bench beside Lotte’s elbow. No steam rose from it. Cold already. She’d been waiting.

“You said you had something,” Sophie said. “Something big.”

Lotte finally looked up. Sophie’s eyes were sharp, hungry. Not for coffee. For the truth.

“Not here,” Lotte said.

“Nowhere is safe.”

“Not even here?”

Sophie smirked, but it didn’t reach her eyes. “You’re the one hiding in a museum.”

Lotte closed the laptop. The screens darkened. She reached for her coat, still damp from the rain outside.

“Come on,” she said. “We can talk on the move.”

Sophie grabbed the coffee cup, tossed it in the trash bin with a practiced flick of her wrist. “Lead the way.”

They stepped into the hallway, the polished floors reflecting the overhead lights like strips of silver. Lotte led Sophie toward the staff stairwell, the one that led down to the service tunnel beneath the building. No cameras there. No prying eyes.

Sophie walked close, her voice low. “You said you had Victor’s files.”

“I said I had *files*.”

“Plural.”

“Yes.”

Sophie’s fingers tapped against her thigh. “Enough to bury him?”

Lotte paused at the stairwell door. Turned to Sophie. “More than enough.”

Sophie’s breath hitched. Just for a second.

The stairwell was narrow, steep, lit by flickering fluorescents. They descended in single file, shoulders brushing the cold concrete walls. At the bottom, a service corridor stretched ahead, the air thick with the scent of cleaning chemicals and old stone.

Lotte stopped outside a storage closet marked *Maintenance – No Entry*. She pulled out a keycard, swiped it.

The door clicked.

Inside, the space was cramped, lined with shelves of spare brushes and solvents. A folding table sat in the center, and on it, a single laptop. The same one from the lab, but now connected to an external drive. The encryption app ran again, the files cycling through their decryption.

Sophie stepped in, her coat brushing the shelves. She pulled a small recorder from her pocket, set it on the table between them.

“Talk,” she said.

Lotte didn’t sit. She leaned against the shelves, arms crossed. “The files aren’t just my forgeries.”

Sophie stiffened. “Whose then?”

Lotte opened the laptop, maximized the window. The file list appeared, each one labeled with a date and a name.

“His,” she said.

Sophie leaned in, her reflection warped in the screen.

Lotte clicked the top file.

A digital scan of a contract appeared. Dated 2019. Between Victor De Vries and a shell company called *Veritas Art Services*. The transaction amount: €2.3 million.

The buyer? A private collector, name redacted.

Lotte scrolled down. At the bottom, in bold: *Authenticity verified by Lotte van der Meer.*

Her monogram appeared as a watermark in the margin.

Sophie’s breath came faster. “You forged this?”

“No,” Lotte said. “He did. He used my name. My reputation. My signature.”

Sophie picked up the recorder, pressed record. “Proof?”

Lotte minimized the file, opened another.

This one was an email thread. From Victor’s personal account. To a Gmail address: *lotte.vdm@protonmail.com*.

Subject: *The Rembrandt project. Final touches.*

The body read: *The sketch is ready. I’ve added the monogram. It’ll pass inspection under UV if they look hard enough. Buy it. Hold it. The provenance will be ironclad in six months. Then we sell.*

Lotte closed the laptop with a snap.

Sophie stared at her. “You have these files where?”

“Everywhere. Cloud. External drives. Hidden nodes.”

“Why didn’t you use them?”

Lotte looked at the recorder. Then at Sophie.

“Because,” she said, “I didn’t know they existed until two days ago.”

Sophie’s thumb hovered over the stop button. “What changed?”

“Victor changed.” Lotte tapped the laptop lid. “He panicked. He thought I still had my old forgeries. He thought I was coming back to claim them. So he sent Daan to make an offer. Immunity. A deal. A lie.”

Sophie’s face darkened. “Daan Kroon? Victor’s assistant?”

Lotte nodded. “He’s the one who told Victor where to find me three years ago. He’s the reason I had to run.”

Sophie exhaled sharply. “So now you have proof he framed *you* to cover *his* crimes.”

“Exactly.”

Sophie picked up the recorder, stopped it. “This is gold. This is *everything*.”

Lotte didn’t smile. “There’s more.”

“More files?”

“No.” Lotte turned the laptop toward Sophie. “More proof.”

She opened the final file.

A high-resolution scan of a Rembrandt sketch. The same one Victor had been hunting. The one Lotte had hidden in her bench at the Met.

The sketch itself was visible. But beneath it, faintly, almost invisible, was a digital layer—a ghost image. A second sketch, overlaid. The monogram—*L* intertwined with a date: 2019.

Victor’s monogram.

Not hers.

Sophie’s fingers hovered over the trackpad. “You’re saying Victor forged this sketch. Using your monogram. Planting it on you.”

“Yes.”

Sophie zoomed in. The monogram was subtle, but undeniable. A forger’s signature, hidden in plain sight.

She looked up at Lotte. “This is the piece he’s using to discredit you. The one he’s hunting.”

“Yes.”

Sophie leaned back, rubbing her temples. “If this gets out—”

“Victor loses everything,” Lotte finished.

Sophie picked up the recorder again, turned it on.

Lotte reached out, stopped her.

“Not yet,” Lotte said.

Sophie frowned. “Why not?”

“Because Victor doesn’t know these files exist. And if he finds out you have them—”

“He’ll bury me,” Sophie finished.

“No.” Lotte’s voice was flat. “He’ll bury *you*.”

Sophie stared at her.

Lotte closed the laptop. “The files aren’t safe with you. Not yet.”

“Then where?”

Lotte reached into her pocket, pulled out a USB drive. Tiny, black. She set it on the table between them.

“With me,” she said. “Until the right moment.”

Sophie’s eyes flicked from the drive to Lotte’s face. “And when is that?”

“The KunstRAI fair.”

Sophie’s eyebrows lifted. “In six weeks?”

Lotte nodded. “That’s when Victor plans to unveil the sketch. That’s when he plans to expose me as a forger. Again.”

Sophie picked up the USB drive, turned it over in her fingers. “You want to drop this at the fair? In front of everyone?”

“No.” Lotte stepped closer. “I want to drop it on Victor. In front of the world.”

Sophie met her gaze. “And if he finds out before then?”

Lotte’s mouth tightened. “Then he’ll know he’s already lost.”

Sophie slipped the USB into her coat pocket. She turned off the recorder, tucked it back into her bag.

“You’re playing a dangerous game,” she said.

Lotte reached for the laptop, closed it. “I’ve been playing it for three years.”

Sophie zipped her bag shut. “I’ll keep the files secure. Offline. Air-gapped.”

“Good.”

“And I’ll keep digging.” Sophie stepped toward the door. “There’s more. I can feel it.”

Lotte nodded. “I know.”

Sophie paused at the threshold, glanced back.

“You’re not afraid?” she asked.

Lotte looked at the folded laptop case on the shelf. Thought of Victor’s voice on the phone. *You’re not as invisible as you think.*

Then she looked at Sophie.

“Fear,” she said, “is for people who have something left to lose.”

Sophie held her gaze for a long moment. Then she slipped out into the corridor, her footsteps fading into the hum of the building’s infrastructure.

Lotte stayed in the closet, listening to the silence.

She picked up the laptop, slipped it into its case.

Then she heard it.

A sound from the corridor. Not footsteps. Not voices.

A beep.

Then another.

An alarm, distant but clear.

The closet’s light flickered.

Lotte’s breath caught.

The museum’s fire suppression system.

Activated.

From the lab above.

Chapter 11

The night air in De Pijp carried the scent of rain and fried food from Albert Cuyp Market’s late stalls. Sophie Laurent sat in the back of a black Renault, engine idling, her laptop open on her thighs. The screen glowed pale in the dim cab, illuminating her face in sharp angles.

On the passenger seat beside her, a burner phone vibrated. She ignored it.

“They’re moving,” she said. Not to anyone in the car—there was no one else. She spoke to the device, to the encrypted line she’d used for years. “Victor’s team just pulled over outside the safe house. Two black vans, no plates.”

The voice on the other end was calm, accented. “You have the files?”

“Yes.”

“Then you’re a target now.”

“I know.”

A pause. Then, quieter: “Sophie, get out. Now.”

She closed the laptop. The screen died. She set it on the seat, spun the burner phone in her palm.

“Can’t,” she said. “Not until it’s over.”

The Renault’s headlights cut through the rain, reflecting off wet cobblestones. She watched the market stall owners rolling up awnings, their movements slow, methodical. Like they were preparing for something.

Her phone buzzed again. Same number. She rejected the call.

The burner lit up once more—text this time. A single word:

*BURN.*

She thumbed the screen to lock it. Swallowed.

Then her personal phone rang. Unknown caller.

She hesitated. Then answered.

“Laurent.”

“Sophie.” Daan Kroon’s voice was rough, like he’d been drinking. Or screaming. Or both. “You have to stop. Now.”

She tightened her grip on the phone. “Or what?”

“Or they’ll put you in a cell next to your source.” His breath hitched. “You know which one.”

Her stomach dropped. *Veritas Art Services.* The shell company that had laundered Victor’s forgeries. The one that had suddenly folded last week—after Sophie’s last article.

“You’re bluffing,” she said.

“Am I?” His voice cracked. “Victor’s got your digital footprint nailed down. IP traces. VPN logs. He’s going to the police in twenty minutes with everything. Hacking. Fraud. Possession of stolen data. He’s spinning it like you’ve been working with a crime syndicate.”

She stared at the rain streaking the windshield. The Renault’s wipers slapped back and forth, a slow, wet rhythm.

“Why are you telling me this?” she asked.

Silence. Then, quieter: “Because I helped build the case against you.”

Her fingers went numb.

He kept talking. “I didn’t know. Not at first. But I—”

“You fed me information,” she cut in. “You knew about Veritas. You gave me the shell company names.”

“I thought it was clean!” His voice rose. “I thought he was buying real art. But he wasn’t. He was selling fakes. And he was using *me* to cover it.”

She closed her eyes. The weight of it settled over her. The source she’d trusted. The man who’d smiled at her in cafés, who’d slipped her documents over wine glasses.

“You’re protecting him,” she said.

“No.” His voice broke. “I’m protecting *you*. From him.”

The line went dead.

She tossed the phone onto the seat. It skittered into the footwell, screen still lit, Daan’s last words frozen in digital ink.

*From him.*

She laughed. A short, bitter sound.

Then her burner buzzed again. Not a call. A notification.

A news alert:

VICTOR DE VRIES NAMED CHAIR OF KUNSTRAI FAIR ORGANIZING COMMITTEE

Her breath stopped.

She opened the link.

The photograph showed Victor at a podium, smiling, shaking hands. The caption read: *De Vries to oversee the 2024 fair, ensuring unparalleled transparency in Dutch art.*

She slammed the Renault’s door open and stumbled out into the rain. The cold soaked through her coat instantly.

She dialed Lotte.

No answer.

She dialed again.

Voicemail.

She left a message, voice low and urgent: “They’re moving on us. Victor’s locking the fair down. He’s making himself untouchable. You need to get the files out. Now.”

She hung up.

The street was emptying. The market stalls were dark. The rain fell harder.

She looked up.

A fairground guard in a bright orange jacket stood across the canal, under a flickering streetlamp. He held something small in his hand—a postcard? A brochure?

Then he tucked it into his pocket and walked away.

She frowned.

Then her burner buzzed—text from an unknown number:

*Check your bag.*

She froze.

She reached into her shoulder bag, pulled out the USB drive.

It was gone. Only the lanyard remained.

She tore the bag open, dumped the contents onto the passenger seat.

Her recorder. Her notebook. A half-eaten protein bar. Nothing else.

The USB was gone.

Her blood turned to ice.

She spun, scanning the street.

Then she saw it.

A figure near the canal path, slipping behind a row of bicycles. Tall. Coat dark. Moving fast.

Sophie broke into a run.

The figure turned the corner onto Eerste van der Helststraat, disappearing into the shadows.

She sprinted after him, rain soaking her hair, blurring her vision.

She skidded to a stop at the corner.

The street was empty.

Only a single object lay on the cobblestones.

A monogrammed sketch.

She picked it up.

The paper was old, yellowed at the edges. The ink was crisp, dark. The monogram in the corner was unmistakable: *L* intertwined with a date.

Her pulse hammered.

She didn’t recognize the sketch. But she knew who did.

Victor De Vries.

She turned it over.

On the back, in pencil, barely legible:

*For Victor. The real thing. The real Lotte.*

Her stomach lurched.

Sophie looked down the empty street. Then at the sketch in her hands.

Then she made a decision.

She tucked it into her coat pocket.

Turned back toward the Renault.

And started running again—this time toward the Rijksmuseum.

---

The canal boat rocked gently beneath Lotte’s feet as she watched the fairground lights glow in the distance. She stood at the stern, wrapped in a black woolen scarf, her breath curling in the cold air.

The boat’s cabin behind her was dark. Empty. She’d left the engine off, the mooring ropes taut.

She checked her watch.

9:47 p.m.

Daan hadn’t texted. Hadn’t called. Hadn’t shown.

She flexed her fingers inside her gloves.

Then her phone lit up.

Unknown number.

She answered.

“You stole my sketch.”

Victor’s voice. Smooth. Smug.

She didn’t react. Just stared at the fairgrounds, where floodlights illuminated the entrance like a stage.

“Which one?” she asked.

“The one I planted on you.” His tone sharpened. “The Rembrandt sketch. The one with your monogram.”

She let silence stretch.

Then: “I don’t have it.”

“Don’t lie.”

“I’m not.” She kept her voice calm. “I burned everything three years ago.”

A pause. Then, quieter: “Then how do I have a copy?”

She didn’t answer.

“Lotte.” His voice dropped. “I have a guard at the fairgrounds. He just handed me a sketch. One he found near De Pijp. One that matches the monogram on the original.”

Her throat tightened.

“He says it looks important,” Victor continued. “Claims it’s worth millions. Says he’s bringing it to me personally.”

She exhaled through her nose.

“You’re lying,” she said.

“I’m not.” His voice was almost gentle. “And when I open it—when I see that monogram—you’ll be finished. Again.”

The line went dead.

She pulled the phone from her ear, stared at the screen.

Then her burner buzzed.

A text from Sophie:

*They’re coming.*

She typed back: *Where?*

*The fairgrounds. Victor’s penthouse.*

She locked the phone, slipped it into her pocket.

The boat creaked as she shifted her weight.

Ahead, the fairground gates loomed, bathed in golden light.

Behind her, the canal rippled, dark and endless.

She made her decision.

She stepped forward.

The boat swung slightly, then steadied as she jumped onto the dock.

She walked toward the city, toward the lights, toward the sound of voices rising in the night.

She didn’t look back.

Chapter 12

Victor’s penthouse smelled of cigar smoke and leather. Lotte stepped over the threshold, her boots clicking against the polished oak floor. The living room stretched before her, all dark wood and low lighting, the city skyline framed in floor-to-ceiling windows. Victor stood by the desk, one hand resting on a closed black leather portfolio.

He didn’t turn. Didn’t speak.

Lotte set the Rembrandt sketch on the desk between them.

Victor’s fingers twitched. Then he looked down.

His face didn’t change. Not at first. Just a slow blink, like he was adjusting to a sudden glare. Then his jaw tightened. His grip on the portfolio whitened.

“You kept them,” he said.

Lotte folded her arms. “You kept mine.”

He lifted the portfolio, tapped the edge against the desk. “You think these are originals?”

“They’re yours,” she said. “The ones you used to frame me.”

Victor exhaled through his nose, a sound like a tired man suppressing laughter. “You’re delusional. Still.”

“Am I?”

He opened the portfolio. Pulled out a single sheet. Held it up. “This is a real Rembrandt sketch. Worth twenty million. Don’t you dare confuse it with your forgeries.”

Lotte didn’t look at it. “You forged it. Just like you forged the Vermeer in ’21. Just like you forged the Hals in ’19. I have the scans. The timestamps. The brushstroke overlays. Every one signed with your monogram.”

Victor didn’t flinch. He set the sketch down, smoothed it with one finger. “You really think anyone will believe you?”

“They will when they see the digital files,” she said. “The ones I uploaded to four major outlets. Live. Right now.”

His eyes flicked to the ceiling, as if he could see the upload in progress. “You hacked into my cloud.”

“No,” Lotte said. “I didn’t need to. Some files just need to be found in the right place at the right time.”

Victor’s smile was thin, practiced. “Noble of you. But no one trusts a forger’s word.”

“They don’t have to trust me,” Lotte said. “They only have to trust the proof. The dates. The signatures. The transfer logs. Victor De Vries’s personal cloud account, synced to his devices—including the one Daan gave him.”

Victor’s fingers stilled.

Lotte watched him closely. “He didn’t know what he was handing over. Just like he didn’t know what he told Sophie last week. Or that he still had a key to my old studio. Or that he kept the backup drives when he burned the rest.”

Victor’s jaw moved. No words.

“You framed me,” Lotte said. “Not the other way around. And now the world will know it.”

Victor reached for his phone on the desk. His screen lit up as it unlocked. He tapped an icon. Watched a bar fill. Waited.

Then he scrolled. Stopped. Tapped again.

His breath hitched.

Lotte didn’t look at his screen. She watched his face instead.

His color drained. Not suddenly—like a tide pulling back. His fingers tightened around the phone. The screen glowed pale on his skin.

A tremor ran through his hand.

He set the phone down.

Lotte didn’t ask. She already knew.

“They’re pulling their bids,” he said quietly. “All of them. The Americans. The Russians. The German syndicates. All gone.”

She said nothing.

Victor lifted his head. His eyes were hollow. “You uploaded the wrong files.”

Lotte tilted her head. “What?”

He tapped the phone. Swiped. Tapped again. “You sent the wrong files. The ones you *think* are mine.”

She felt a flicker of doubt. Just a spark.

Victor’s voice hardened. “You uploaded the forgeries. The ones *you* made. Not the ones *I* made. Not the ones that prove *my* fraud. Just the ones that prove you’re still a liar.”

Lotte stepped closer. Looked at the screen.

The files were labeled. *Veritas Series A. Veritas Series B. Veritas Series C.*

She recognized the filenames. Recognized the forgeries.

Her stomach dropped.

Victor stood up straight. “You played right into my hands.”

Lotte’s pulse hammered in her throat. She thought of Sophie’s lost USB. Of Daan’s warnings. Of the sketch Sophie had found in the rain.

Victor’s lips curled. “You sent *your* work. Not mine. And now the world thinks it was all a setup. That you faked the evidence against me. That you’re trying to frame *me* to cover your own crimes.”

He reached past her. Grabbed a remote from the desk. Pressed a button.

The penthouse TV turned on. A news ticker scrolled across the bottom. A breaking alert:

*DUTCH PRESS HACKED? ART SCANDAL ROCKS AUCTION WORLD*

Victor’s voice dropped to a purr. “They’re calling it a smear campaign. A desperate attempt by a disgraced forger to regain relevance. You’ve handed me the narrative, Lotte. And it’s a good one.”

She swallowed. “The files I sent—”

“Are all yours,” he said. “Clean. Authentic. Watermarked. No room for doubt.”

Lotte’s mind raced. Three years of files. Stored. Hidden. Backed up. She had sent the wrong ones.

Victor stepped around the desk. Close enough that she could smell his cologne—something sharp and expensive.

He leaned in. “You wanted to destroy me. But you only destroyed yourself.”

Footsteps echoed in the hallway. The penthouse door swung open.

Daan stood in the doorway, coat dripping rain, eyes wide. His gaze flicked between them.

Victor didn’t turn. “You’re late.”

Daan’s breath was uneven. “I know.”

“You brought the files?”

Daan hesitated. Then pulled a USB drive from his pocket. Handed it to Victor.

Victor took it. Held it up between two fingers. “This is the real one.”

Daan nodded.

Victor smirked. “Good.”

Lotte watched Daan. He wouldn’t meet her eyes.

Victor dropped the USB into his desk drawer. Closed it.

He turned to Lotte. “You should go.”

She didn’t move. “We’re not done.”

“We are,” Victor said. “Because you just gave the world exactly what I wanted them to see.”

Outside, rain tapped against the windows. Distant sirens wailed.

Daan shifted. “Lotte—”

She cut him off with a glance. Then she turned and walked to the door.

Victor called after her. “You’ll be hearing from the police. And the association. And every gallery that ever trusted you.”

She paused. Didn’t look back.

Then she left.

The hallway was empty. The elevator doors waiting.

She pressed the call button.

The doors slid open.

She stepped inside.

As the doors closed, she pulled out her phone. Swiped. Checked the upload status.

One bar left.

The files were still broadcasting.

She exited the lift on the ground floor. Stepped into the marble lobby. The doorman nodded. She nodded back.

She walked into the rain.

Her burner buzzed. Unknown number.

She answered.

“You were always my best,” Victor’s voice said, calm and cold. “Even when you worked against me.”

She stopped under the awning of a closed gallery. The rain hissed on the pavement.

“But,” he continued, “I was your only teacher. And that means everything.”

The line went dead.

Lotte lowered the phone. Stared at the wet street.

Then her personal phone buzzed.

Daan.

She ignored it.

She tucked the phone away. Walked into the rain.

Chapter 13

Victor sat on a low leather sofa in a soundproof studio on the Nieuwendam waterfront. The walls were bare except for a single framed print—a 1970s abstract piece that should have belonged in a municipal office, not a confession booth. A ring light haloed his face. A producer in a headset counted down on her fingers.

Five.

Four.

Three.

Victor exhaled, adjusted his glasses, and pasted on a smile that looked like it had been ironed on.

The red light turned green.

“Victor De Vries,” the anchor said, her voice warm, practiced. “Thank you for joining us this evening. Last week’s art forgery scandal involving the Rijksmuseum restoration lab has sent shockwaves through the international art world. You’ve been at the center of it all. Tell us—what happened?”

Victor folded his hands in his lap. “A deliberate smear campaign,” he said. “A person seeking to regain relevance through deception. Someone with a history. A troubled past.”

The anchor leaned forward. “You’re not naming names.”

“Not necessary,” Victor said. “But what *is* necessary is restoring trust. The Dutch Art Dealers Association takes full responsibility for any oversight. We will cooperate fully with investigators. And we will ensure that the integrity of the market remains unblemished.”

A beat of silence. The anchor’s smile didn’t waver. “So you’re telling viewers that the forgeries were real?”

Victor’s fingers twitched. “I’m telling viewers that the *accusations* were fabricated. That a disgraced figure from the past is attempting to destabilize an entire industry through misinformation.”

The producer gave a small, urgent wave.

Victor continued. “I’ve spent my career building a transparent, ethical art world. That won’t change. But we must also address the truth: not all forgeries come from outside the system. Sometimes, the threat is internal.”

The anchor blinked. “Are you suggesting someone within your own organization is responsible?”

Victor’s gaze remained steady. “I’m suggesting that trust, once broken, is hard to repair. And that vigilance must be constant.”

The producer signaled the end. Victor stood, smoothed his jacket, and nodded to the camera before the light cut out.

He stepped off the set and into a narrow hallway lit by fluorescent tubes. The air smelled of coffee and old carpet. Two assistants hovered with a tablet, waiting to debrief him on social media response.

Victor waved them off. “Later.”

He took the back stairs down to the parking garage, his shoes clicking on concrete. The night was cool, damp. A thin rain misted the windshield of his black Mercedes. He slid behind the wheel and sat for a moment, staring at the rain-streaked glass.

The phone in his pocket buzzed. Private number.

Victor answered. “Yes.”

Daan’s voice was tight. “You just lied on live TV.”

Victor turned the key in the ignition. The engine purred to life. “I clarified the record.”

“You made her a martyr.”

Victor adjusted the rearview mirror. “No. She did that herself. By uploading her own files. By admitting to forgeries she thought were hers. She handed me the narrative.”

A pause. Then Daan’s breath hitched. “You knew.”

Victor didn’t answer.

Daan’s voice dropped. “You *planned* this.”

Victor shifted into drive. “Plans change, Daan. But outcomes don’t. Not when you understand people.”

“You set her up.”

“She set *herself* up,” Victor said. “Again. Just like before. But this time, the world sees it. The police. The association. The press. She’s finished.”

Daan didn’t speak.

Victor pulled out of the garage and into the canal-lit streets. The city felt smaller tonight, tighter. Controlled.

“You’re quiet,” Victor said. “I expected more from you.”

“What do you want from me?”

“Loyalty,” Victor said. “That’s all I’ve ever wanted.”

Daan exhaled. “I can’t do this anymore.”

Victor turned onto Prins Hendrikkade. The lights of the city shimmered on the black water. “You already are doing it. Just not with the courage I need.”

“I’m done,” Daan said. “I quit.”

Victor smiled. “You don’t quit. You disappear. And right now, you’re too visible.”

“Then what?”

“Go home,” Victor said. “Drink. Regret. Do whatever it is you do when you think no one’s watching. But remember this: you gave me the files. You saved the market from contamination. That counts for something.”

Daan didn’t answer.

Victor ended the call. Tossed the phone onto the passenger seat.

He parked along the Reguliersgracht, near a row of narrow houseboats. The rain had stopped, but the cobblestones glistened. He walked to the door of a discreet bar called *De Drie Hendriken*—The Three Henries—a place where collectors and critics drank under the radar.

Inside, the air was thick with smoke and the scent of aged whiskey. A single lantern hung over the bar. Two men in dark suits played chess in the corner. The bartender nodded as Victor sat on a stool.

Victor ordered a single malt, neat. The glass arrived with a splash of water. He swirled it, let the amber catch the light.

He hadn’t expected to be here. Not tonight. Not like this.

His phone buzzed again. Unknown number.

He stared at the screen. Then answered.

“Victor.”

It wasn’t Daan. It was Sophie Laurent.

Her voice was smooth, almost amused. “I hear you just declared war on a ghost.”

Victor took a sip. The whiskey burned clean. “I declared the truth.”

“Funny,” Sophie said. “I just got off the phone with Sophie. She said you’re the one who’s been trafficking stolen art through your restorers.”

Victor set the glass down. “She’s lying.”

“Is she?” Sophie asked. “Funny how the files she showed me were the same ones Lotte uploaded. Funny how your monogram was all over them. Funny how your cloud was the original source.”

Victor’s fingers tightened around the glass.

Sophie continued. “She also said you forged your own Rembrandt to frame her. Said you’ve been doing it for years. Said you even kept her forgeries to pin on others.”

Victor didn’t speak.

Sophie’s laugh was soft. “You really thought no one would notice?”

The line went dead.

Victor stared at the phone. The screen dimmed. Locked.

He set it down. Picked up the glass. Drank.

Then his personal phone buzzed. Daan’s name flashed on the screen.

Victor ignored it.

He signaled the bartender for another drink.

Outside, the rain began again. Gentle. Persistent. Like time itself.

Chapter 14

The *De Kas*, an unmarked restaurant behind the Rijksmuseum, smelled of roasted chicory and seared duck. Lotte sat at the back booth, arms crossed, watching the door. The table between them held two coffees and a plate of stale stroopwafels.

Sophie Laurent arrived five minutes late, coat damp from the evening drizzle, hair tucked behind her ears. She didn’t apologize. Just slid in, shrugged off her scarf, and reached for a waffle.

“You look like you’ve been sleeping in museums,” Sophie said.

Lotte didn’t smile. “You look like you’re still chasing a story you’ll never publish.”

Sophie tore the waffel in half. “Funny. That’s exactly what Victor said about me this afternoon.”

A busboy passed with a tray of mussels. The clatter filled the silence.

Lotte leaned forward. “You have the files?”

Sophie wiped crumbs from her fingers. “I have *parts* of them. The parts that implicate Victor in human trafficking, money laundering, and, oh yes—art forgery. But here’s the thing.” She tapped the table. “He’s already discredited them. Calls them doctored, planted, another smear job by a disgraced forger.”

Lotte glanced at the door. No one noticed them. Good.

“You’re not telling me something,” Lotte said.

Sophie met her eyes. “I stopped believing in heroes a long time ago, Lotte. But I still believe in leverage.” She slid a folded sheet across the table. “This is the offer. Never mention the lab in Poland.”

*I, Lotte van der Meer, was the principal forger for the Dutch Art Dealer’s Association under Victor De Vries, falsifying works by Rembrandt, Vermeer, and Hals. My mentor taught me everything I know.*

Nothing about the files. Nothing about Daan.

“You want me to sign my life away,” Lotte said.

“No,” Sophie said, pulling out a pen. “I want you to sign it away *publicly*. Tonight.”

Lotte picked up the pen. Tapped it against the table.

“Why now?”

Sophie exhaled through her nose. “Because Victor just aired a special. Said you hacked your own files to frame him. Said you’re a desperate woman with a grudge. And because,” she leaned in, “my editor is about to run a piece about *me*—claims I fabricated sources, bribed restorers, even forged documents to get ahead. Cute, right? Two liars in one city.”

Lotte set the pen down.

Sophie’s mouth twisted. “You think you’re the only one who knows how to play this game?”

Lotte reached for her coffee. The ceramic was still warm.

“Victor’s going down,” Lotte said. “But not like this. Not through a tabloid confession.”

“Then how?”

“Through proof. Undeniable proof. Something even Victor can’t spin.” Lotte’s fingers curled around the rim. “A Rembrandt sketch. One he forged himself. One that still carries *my* monogram.”

Sophie’s eyebrows lifted. “You’re joking.”

“It’s in a private collection. Owned by a man who won’t talk to police. But he’ll talk to me.” Lotte met her gaze. “I restore it. I date it. I release the images. Victor’s career ends in an instant.”

Sophie stared at her. Then laughed—a sharp, disbelieving sound.

“You’re serious.”

Lotte nodded.

“And what do *you* get out of it?”

“My name back,” Lotte said. “A chance to work again. No more whispers. No more hiding.” She tapped the confession paper. “I sign *that* after the sketch is public. Not before.”

Sophie considered it. Then shook her head. “No. We go together. Article and sketch, simultaneous release. One feeds the other.”

“No,” Lotte said. “If you run first, Victor buries the sketch. If I run first, he tries to destroy it. We need separation. A gap. A moment where the world believes me before the confession.”

Sophie exhaled slowly. “You’re asking for my trust.”

“You’re asking for my life,” Lotte said. “Take the deal or walk.”

Sophie picked up the pen. Flipped it between her fingers.

“What’s your timeline?”

“Three days. The sketch is at the safe deposit vault on Herengracht. I need access. I need silence.” Lotte slid a key across the table. “This opens it. The vault is yours after 10 AM tomorrow. But you don’t touch the sketch. You don’t open it. You don’t even *see* it until I say so.”

Sophie picked up the key. Held it up to the light.

“You’re still protecting someone,” she said.

Lotte didn’t answer.

Sophie pocketed the key. “Fine. Three days. But if this is a trick—”

“It’s not,” Lotte said.

Sophie stood, draped her scarf over her shoulders. “I’ll send you the draft article tonight. One chance to edit. One chance to walk away.”

Lotte watched her go. The door chimed, let in a gust of rain, then sealed shut.

She sat alone, the coffee cooling, the stroopwafels forgotten.

Then the back door of the restaurant creaked open.

Daan.

He wore a dark overcoat, damp at the shoulders, hands shoved in his pockets. His face was thinner. His eyes red-rimmed.

He didn’t speak. Just stood there, blocking the exit.

Lotte didn’t move. “You followed me.”

“No.” He stepped closer. “I *found* you.”

She looked away. “The museum lab has cameras.”

“They’re in the ceiling. Not in the stalls.” He pulled out a chair, sat without waiting for permission. “I needed to see you.”

“You saw me on live TV,” she said. “You saw me labeled a liar, a criminal, a ghost.”

“That wasn’t me,” he said. “That was Victor. That was—”

“That was *your* silence,” she said. “That was you giving him the files. That was you watching me burn.”

Daan’s jaw tightened. “I didn’t know he’d use them like that.”

“You didn’t *want* to know,” she said. “You wanted plausible deniability. You wanted to keep your job. Your apartment. Your life.”

He didn’t deny it.

She drank the cold coffee. Made a face.

“What do you want, Daan?”

He reached into his coat. Pulled out a folded document. Slid it across.

A resignation letter. Dated today. Addressed to Victor De Vries.

“My name’s not on it,” Daan said. “No mention of you. No mention of the past. Just one line: *I resign, effective immediately.*”

Lotte didn’t touch it.

“And?”

“And I ask for one thing,” he said. “One chance to make it right. Not for you. For me.”

She looked at the letter. The ink was fresh.

“How?”

“I know a lab,” he said. “An old friend in Groningen. Six hours north. No cameras. No records. No one to ask questions. I can disappear there. Work in peace. Restore damaged pieces. Never speak of Amsterdam again.”

Lotte picked up the letter. Turned it over.

“Why tell me?”

“Because I hurt you,” he said. “And because, even now, I still love you.”

The words hung in the air like cigarette smoke.

She folded the letter slowly. Tucked it into her pocket.

“You want anonymity,” she said. “I’ll give it to you. But not in Groningen.”

Daan’s eyes flickered. “Where, then?”

“Here,” she said. “In Amsterdam. But underground. In the archives. The basement vault of the Rijksmuseum. No one goes there. No cameras. Not even the cleaners. You can work. You can stay. And you can never be found.”

Daan stared at her.

“Why?” he asked.

She stood. Slid her coat on.

“Because you were the only one who ever believed in me,” she said. “And that counts for something.”

He didn’t reach for her. Didn’t move.

She walked past him, out the back, into the rain.

The door swung shut behind her.

Daan sat at the table, alone, the resignation letter still in his pocket.

Then his phone buzzed.

A message from Sophie:

*Article goes live at midnight. Title: “The Forger Who Knew Too Much.” You coming or not?*

He stared at the screen.

Then he pocketed the phone.

And walked out into the Amsterdam night.

Chapter 15

The Rijksmuseum’s main hall stretched beneath a vaulted ceiling painted with golden cherubs and battles lost centuries ago. Sunlight slanted through the glass dome, stroking the marble floors, igniting the gilt frames of the Dutch Masters. Tourists murmured, cameras snapping like impatient cicadas.

Lotte adjusted the gloves. Her fingers, steady as a metronome’s beat, hovered over the canvas. The Rembrandt attribution had been reinstated after Victor’s arrest—justice served, though the irony of it still tasted bitter on her tongue.

Across the hall, a security guard adjusted his earpiece, nodding at a tour guide. No one looked at her twice. She was just another restorer, another dutiful hand in the grand machinery of the museum.

Then the cameras shifted.

A uniformed officer appeared at the entrance of the hall, scanning the room with practiced calm. He nodded at the guard, who tensed but didn’t react. The officer moved forward, eyes locked on Lotte.

She didn’t stop working. The brush moved in slow arcs, blending pigment into the illusion of age and authenticity.

The officer stopped beside her. “Lotte van der Meer?”

She didn’t look up. “Yes.”

“You’re to come with me.”

A murmur rippled through the tourists. The officer’s hand rested just above his holster.

Lotte set the brush down. The canvas held her monogram, invisible to the untrained eye, but hers nonetheless.

“Now?” she asked.

“Yes.”

From his pocket, he produced a folded paper. “Court order. Victor De Vries requests your presence in the detention center. He’s sketching.”

Lotte’s breath hitched before she could stop it. The air in the hall thickened.

She stood. Removed her gloves. Folded them neatly.

“Of course,” she said. “How generous of him.”

The officer led the way. The guard didn’t meet her eyes.

---

The detention center smelled of mildew and antiseptic. Fluorescent lights buzzed like trapped wasps. Lotte walked down the corridor, her heels clicking against the tile, her posture unbroken.

Victor’s cell was at the end. A small camera blinked above the door.

The officer swiped his card. The lock disengaged with a mechanical sigh.

Victor sat at a metal table, leaning over a sketchpad. His orange jumpsuit strained at the shoulders. A pencil moved with controlled precision across the paper.

He didn’t look up.

Lotte stepped inside. The door sealed shut behind her.

Victor continued sketching. “Took you long enough,” he said.

“Traffic,” she replied.

He chuckled, low and familiar. “Always the smart one.”

The sketchpad lay between them. A face emerged from the strokes—a Rembrandt self-portrait, unmistakable. But the eyes weren’t his. They were hers.

Lotte’s stomach clenched.

Victor finally lifted his head. His smile was a blade unsheathed. “Recognize it?”

She didn’t touch the paper. Didn’t flinch.

“No,” she said.

“Liar,” he said. “Just like always.”

The guard lingered in the doorway, impatient. Lotte ignored him.

Victor rotated the sketchpad slightly. The monogram was there, tucked into the corner of the sheet—Lotte’s signature, not his.

Her breath came shallow.

“You still sign your work,” he said. “Even when you don’t mean to.”

Lotte met his gaze. His eyes were cold, calculating. Victor De Vries had spent years building an empire on lies, but he couldn’t erase her from the canvas—no more than he could erase her from the past.

“What do you want?” she asked.

He set the pencil down. “To remind you,” he said, “that I built this world. I decide who stays in the light and who rots in the dark. Even from here.”

Lotte looked at the sketch. The monogram pulsed like a heartbeat.

“You’re not in the light anymore,” she said.

Victor leaned back. “No. But you are.”

The guard shifted. “Time’s up.”

Lotte turned to leave. Her hand brushed the sketchpad.

Victor’s voice dropped to a whisper. “You’ll always be my greatest work, Lotte.”

She walked out without looking back.

The corridor stretched ahead, endless and sterile. The air tasted like defeat and ozone.

At the end, the sunlight waited.
